## A Day in a Life of Huge Treatment Effects

Your morning starts with wild swings in trust for your spouse: up 11% from holding a warm cup of coffee, [@williams2008] then down 30% from the smell of last night's kitchen trash. [@lee2012] Uncertain about your marriage, you pick out a red shirt for the 10% bump on the attractiveness scale [@elliot2008] and head to work. There a power pose jacks your testosterone 30%, and putting your feet up on the desk makes you 43% more likely to take a risky bet. [@carney2010] Your inevitable meeting with HR is all but guaranteed by a dim room light that made you cheat on your reimbursement forms 47% above baseline. [@zhong2010lamps] To save your job and protect your health, you duck out of work early and head to the bar. The bottle of red wine reduces your cardiovascular mortality 30% and your all-cause mortality 20%. [@ronksley2011] Somehow you make it home and vow to do better the next day. You swallow a fistful of vitamin C, [@pauling1970] echinacea, [@shah2007] zinc lozenges, [@prasad2008] and melatonin, [@petrie1989] all guaranteed to slash your colds and jet lag. Exhausted, you doze off to a podcast about there being yet another study proving the existence of ESP. [@bem2011]

## Huge Silly Treatment Effects are Hallucinations from a Broken Scientific Publishing Industry {#the-publication-boundary}

Why does science keep popularizing obviously absurd claims about trivial things having large impacts? At least two reasons.

The first you've probably heard of: the *file drawer*. Researchers run many studies and only publish the ones that cross an arbitrary threshold of statistical significance. That's the diagonal line in @fig-publication-boundary. Studies below it mostly never appear; studies above it might. The bigger the sample size *N* (along the horizontal axis), the easier that threshold is to clear.

The second you might not have noticed. Small-*N* studies *cannot produce small-effect publications*. The arithmetic won't allow it. A study with *N* = 40 has to report *d* ≥ 0.62 (Cohen's *d*---treatment effect over outcome SD) to reach *p* \< 0.05, regardless of what's actually true about the world. So when small-*N* studies do publish, they publish huge effects---not because huge effects are there, but because nothing smaller can get through. The intro findings plotted here all sit in the top-left quadrant: unlikely effect sizes propped up by weak evidence.

![The p < .05 publication boundary. For a two-sample test, a study only clears the conventional threshold when observed D is roughly 2 * 1.96 / sqrt(N). The labeled points are the opening-scenario examples that map cleanly onto a single-study two-sample-style D-vs-N point.](_generated/figures/figure1_publication_boundary.png){#fig-publication-boundary}

## It\'s Not Just the Silly Treatments --- Most Huge Effects are Hallucinations {#small-n-hallucinations-are-unhelpful-and-irredeemable}

Below are 1,697 recovered original/replication endpoint pairs, now using the expanded local replication-pair build rather than the smaller draft table alone.

The pairs come from the major coordinated replication efforts: the Reproducibility Project: Psychology, Many Labs 1--5, the Social Sciences and Experimental Economics Replication Projects, the Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology, DARPA SCORE, the Cova experimental philosophy replication, the Pipeline Project, and the Registered Replication Reports.

@fig-replication-combined summarizes the current replication build. 79% of effects shrink, 40% shrink below the p \< 0.05 boundary at the replication sample size, and the fitted log-log slope implies about 49% lower D for each 10x increase in N.

![Huge effect sizes evaporate in larger replication attempts. The current recovered pair table contains 1,697 original/replication endpoint pairs; 79% shrink, 40% shrink below the p < .05 boundary, and the log-log fit implies about 49% lower D for each 10x increase in N. The marginal distributions replace the draft's old separate before/after distribution figure.](_generated/figures/figure2_replication_combined.png){#fig-replication-combined}

::: {.callout .blue}
**Selection criteria for Figure 2.** We keep only recovered original/replication effect pairs where both sides have a usable common-metric effect size on our shared $D$ axis, both sample sizes are known and at least 10, and the replication attempt is larger than the original ($N_r > N_o$). We also collapse obvious site-level repeats to one row per original paper × replication study × endpoint anchor, using summed replication $N$ and an $N$-weighted replication $D$. The unit here is therefore a paper-endpoint pair, not a paper: the current 1,697-row table comes from 974 unique original papers, and 263 of those originals contribute more than one row. That many-to-one structure is expected because one original paper can contribute several claims or outcomes, and some claims are re-tested by multiple labs or samples.
:::

::: {.callout .teal}
**Why use $D$ here, and what are its limits?** We use a shared $D$ axis because Figure 2 needs one magnitude scale that can compare many different literatures and endpoint types. Raw percent changes, relative improvements, and other denominator-sensitive summaries often explode when the baseline is tiny, so they are not stable enough for a cross-study distribution. A standardized effect like $D$ is a cleaner common axis for asking one narrow question: how large did the original claim look, and how large did the larger follow-up look, on the same scale? But $D$ is not neutral. It can be inflated when the pooled SD is unusually small, noisy, range-restricted, or measured in a selected subgroup. It also throws away natural units and can blur baseline risk or clinical importance. And when $D$ is back-converted from odds ratios, hazard ratios, cluster-adjusted models, or adjusted mean differences, the conversion adds another layer of assumptions. So we use $D$ here because it is the least bad common axis for this figure, not because it is a perfect one.
:::

The originals are not approximations to the truth that better statistics could clean up. They are filter artifacts: numbers the publication boundary selected into existence. There's nothing reanalysis can do. The only remedy is to rerun at large *N*.

When you read the next small-*N* paper claiming *d* = 0.8 from *N* = 40, this is what you're looking at.

## Most Effect Sizes Must be Small

The right question is not whether a published estimate crossed *p* < .05. The right question is: given what real effects usually look like, and given that this estimate survived a publication process that rewards large significant estimates, what should I now believe about the true effect?

The first input to that judgment is empirical, not philosophical. **@fig-replication-combined** gives us 1,697 recovered original/replication endpoint pairs, all on a common $D$ scale, all with both sample sizes known, and all with the replication larger than the original. In that sample, 79% of effects shrink, 40% shrink below the original *p* < .05 boundary at the replication sample size, and the fitted log-log relationship implies about 49% lower $D$ for each 10x increase in $N$. That distribution is not an oracle, but it is the least-filtered empirical prior available here: larger samples, direct retests, and far less room for the original publication boundary to select the observed magnitude.

The second input is structural. Appendix F lays out eleven reasons most effects must be small before we ever look at a *p*-value. The short version is that behavioral, biomedical, educational, and social outcomes are bounded noisy aggregates. They are produced by many causes, mediated through partial mechanisms, measured with finite reliability, diluted by active controls and counterfactual exposure, averaged across heterogeneous people and settings, and eroded by site-to-site voltage drop and scale-up decay [@tosh2024; @pearl2001mediation; @mackinnon2002mediation; @spearman1904; @hedge2018reliability; @bryan2021heterogeneity; @klein2018; @alubaydli2017scalability; @pereira2012largeeffects]. Ordinary interventions usually push on one weak edge in a dense causal graph, not on a single unconstrained bottleneck.

The third input is statistical. A published estimate is not just a noisy draw from the world. It is a noisy draw that survived a significance filter. Gelman and Carlin's Type M framework, van Zwet and Cator's significance-filter analysis, and the broader winner's-curse literature all imply the same direction of correction: conditional on significance, small noisy studies exaggerate magnitude [@gelman2014beyondpower; @zwet2021; @ioannidis2008inflation; @loken2017measurement]. So a rational reader needs shrinkage, not just standard errors.

That is what **@fig-prior-comparison** is for. The flat line is the implicit no-shrinkage prior: every effect from $d = 0.01$ to $d = 1,000$ treated as no less plausible than every other. The blue curve is the weakly informative Normal$(0, 0.35)$ prior. It does not ban large effects. It says they are rare enough to pay an evidentiary tax. About 95% of that prior lies inside $|d| < 0.69$. That is a reasonable public-facing approximation to what the replication distribution is already telling us.

![What you implicitly believe before the data. The flat/no-shrinkage line treats large effects as no less plausible than small effects; the Normal(0, 0.35) prior puts most mass on behavioral-science effects near zero. The lower panel shows the linear-scale distribution of larger-N replication effect sizes, with the median marked.](_generated/figures/figure4_prior_comparison.png){#fig-prior-comparison}

Write the update the simple way:

$$
d \sim N(0, \tau^2), \qquad \hat d \mid d, s \sim N(d, s^2)
$$

$$
E[d \mid \hat d, s] = \frac{\tau^2}{\tau^2 + s^2} \hat d
$$

with $\tau = 0.35$. For a balanced two-group design, a useful approximation is $s \approx 2 / \sqrt{N}$. That makes the reader-facing lesson immediate: large studies have small $s$, so the data dominate; small studies have large $s$, so the estimate shrinks hard toward zero. The prior does not make large effects impossible. It makes small-$N$ large effects earn their keep.

Take the kind of result this essay keeps attacking: a 40-person study reporting $\hat d = 0.80$. Then $s \approx 2 / \sqrt{40} \approx 0.316$. Under the Normal$(0, 0.35)$ prior, the shrinkage weight is

$$
w = \frac{0.35^2}{0.35^2 + 0.316^2} \approx 0.55
$$

so the posterior mean is not $0.80$. It is about $0.44$. Run the same observed estimate at $N = 400$, where $s \approx 0.10$, and the weight rises to about 0.92, giving a posterior mean of about $0.74$. That is the whole argument in one contrast. The prior is not saying “big effects never happen.” It is saying “a huge effect from a tiny study is mostly evidence about noise plus selection unless the underlying causal story is extraordinary.”

And that is exactly what the replication evidence shows. Small-$N$ literatures produce large selected estimates; larger replication attempts pull them back toward the effect-size range the world actually allows. Our Figure 2 estimate --- 79% shrinking, 40% shrinking below the original significance boundary, and about 49% lower $D$ per 10x increase in $N$ --- is not a side fact. It is the empirical prior the rest of the paper is asking the reader to use.

Real large effects still exist. But when they are real, they look like vaccines, antibiotics, or other interventions whose targets visibly collapse under coarse measurement and large-scale replication, not like vague primes in 42 undergraduates. That is why the burden of evidence rises with the size of the claim. If a result is supposed to live in the thin tail of @fig-prior-comparison, then the evidence has to look tail-worthy too: large samples, hard outcomes, active controls, repeated success across sites, or effects obvious enough to see without statistical heroics.

## A World In Which Most Published Research Was True Would Be A Nightmare Hellscape {#imagine-life-if-you-could-believe-small-n-work}

Take the published literature at face value for a moment. Suppose large effect sizes did not require large evidence, and a 40-person undergraduate study reporting *d* = 0.8 were about as informative about reality as a 40,000-person preregistered RCT reporting the same thing. In that world, sample size would be a cost consideration, not an epistemic one.

But then this is not just a cloud of papers. It is a map of a world.

What would the published record look like in that world? @fig-published-d-vs-n shows 10,507 published-original candidate papers across 26 source corpora, with each paper represented by its median $D$ and median $N$ in the top panel and the same published paper-level estimates summarized underneath.

![The small-N and huge-effect-size pattern in published journal articles. The top panel shows 10,507 published-original candidate papers across 26 source corpora as paper/unit medians; the lower panel shows the distribution of those same published paper/unit estimates. The paper-level median D is 0.42, median N is 160, and 83% sit above the p <= .10 boundary.](_generated/figures/figure3_published_d_vs_n.png){#fig-published-d-vs-n}

In this corpus, 76.5% of papers have median $D \ge 0.2$, 41.8% have median $D \ge 0.5$, and 21.7% have median $D \ge 0.8$. That is 8,040 nontrivial effects, 4,390 medium-or-larger effects, and 2,281 conventionally large effects. Read literally, the literature is not saying that large effects sometimes happen. It is saying that large effects are ordinary.

But 2,281 is not the implication. It is only the sample count. If this published distribution were a truthful window into the indexed scientific literature, it would scale into a claim about hundreds of thousands to millions of large-effect papers.

Let $J$ be the number of journal articles in the indexed literature and $q$ the fraction of them that are relevant empirical effect-estimating papers. Taking Crossref's roughly 123.1 million journal DOI records and the current 21.7% large-effect share as an order-of-magnitude benchmark, the implied number of paper-level $D \ge 0.8$ claims is

$$
L_{0.8} = 0.217 q J.
$$

| Universe assumption | Relevant empirical / effect papers | Implied $D \ge 0.8$ paper-level large-effect claims |
| --- | ---: | ---: |
| Crossref journal records, only 2% relevant | 2,462,000 | 534,484 |
| Crossref journal records, only 4% relevant | 4,924,000 | 1,068,968 |
| Crossref journal records, 10% relevant | 12,310,000 | 2,672,419 |
| Crossref journal records, 25% relevant | 30,775,000 | 6,681,048 |
| 2023 worldwide S&E output, 10% relevant | 330,000 / year | 71,641 / year |
| 2023 worldwide S&E output, 25% relevant | 825,000 / year | 179,102 / year |

The “millions” claim does not require pretending that all science is psychology or medicine. If only 4% of indexed journal articles are in the relevant class, the published distribution implies about 1,068,968 paper-level $D \ge 0.8$ claims. If 10% are relevant, it implies about 2,672,419. If 25% are relevant, it implies about 6,681,048. At the annual scale, even a 10% relevant-share assumption implies roughly 71,641 new conventionally large effects per year.

Some of those papers are studying the same underlying effect, so paper-level claims are not the same thing as unique causal levers. But duplication has to be enormous to save the literal interpretation. If each genuine large effect generated five papers, the 10% Crossref scenario still implies about 534,484 unique $D \ge 0.8$ effects, and the 25% scenario still implies about 1,336,210. The exact duplication correction is unknown. The scale is the point.

That is a data-generating-process claim, not just a bibliometric one. For a balanced two-group comparison,

$$
r = \frac{d}{\sqrt{d^2 + 4}}, \qquad R^2 = \frac{d^2}{d^2 + 4}.
$$

So a $d = 0.2$ effect corresponds to about $r = 0.10$ and explains about 1.0% of outcome variance. A $d = 0.5$ effect corresponds to about $r = 0.24$ and explains about 5.9% of outcome variance. A $d = 0.8$ effect corresponds to about $r = 0.37$ and explains about 13.8% of outcome variance. Roughly one hundred independent $d = 0.2$ levers, seventeen independent $d = 0.5$ levers, or seven independent $d = 0.8$ levers pointed at the same outcome would use up essentially the whole variance budget. The exact arithmetic changes with dependence, heterogeneity, and measurement, but the qualitative point does not: you cannot have a world in which every small intervention is a giant intervention. The variance has to come from somewhere.

Nor is $d = 0.8$ a subtle effect. Under the usual equal-variance model,

$$
P(Y_T > Y_C) = \Phi(d / \sqrt{2}).
$$

At $d = 0.5$, a random treated observation beats a random control observation about 64% of the time. At $d = 0.8$, it is about 71%. One large effect is survivable. But it is no longer a nudge. It is a major axis of the outcome.

The problem is accumulation. In an unbounded additive model, let

$$
Y = dT + \epsilon,
$$

with balanced treatment assignment and $\epsilon$ variance fixed at 1. Then one $d = 0.8$ lever raises the outcome variance from 1 to $1 + .25d^2 = 1.16$, so the outcome SD rises by about 7.7%. But with $K$ independent $d = 0.8$ levers,

$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y) = 1 + 0.16K.
$$

| Independent $d = 0.8$ levers | New SD if residual SD stays fixed | Apparent $d$ of each original lever after variance inflation |
| ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 1 | 1.08x | 0.74 |
| 5 | 1.34x | 0.60 |
| 10 | 1.61x | 0.50 |
| 20 | 2.05x | 0.39 |
| 50 | 3.00x | 0.27 |
| 100 | 4.12x | 0.19 |

That table is the DGP problem in miniature. If you keep adding real independent $d = 0.8$ effects, either the outcome variance balloons or each effect stops being $d = 0.8$ relative to the new world. You cannot have thousands of simultaneously real, independent, population-level $d = 0.8$ levers on ordinary stable outcomes.

Most real outcomes do not have arbitrary variance. Test scores, depression scales, blood pressure, turnout, BMI, cholesterol, trust ratings, and reaction times have empirically stable distributions. In that fixed-variance world, a large effect does not inflate the outcome. It spends the variance budget instead. A $d = 0.8$ causal variable explaining 13.8% of outcome variance is not “one more thing that matters.” It is one of the main things that matters. Seven independent effects that large exhaust almost the whole budget. Seventeen $d = 0.5$ effects do the same. In a fixed-variance world, a large effect is not a nudge. It is a pillar of the DGP.

The same point looks even harsher for binary outcomes. Using the standard logistic approximation,

$$
\log(OR) \approx d \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}.
$$

So $d = 0.8$ corresponds to an odds ratio of about 4.27. Start with a 10% event probability and apply repeated independent $d = 0.8$ levers:

| Number of independent $d = 0.8$ levers | Probability after applying $OR \approx 4.27$ each |
| ---: | ---: |
| 1 | 32.17% |
| 2 | 66.93% |
| 3 | 89.62% |
| 4 | 97.36% |
| 7 | 99.97% |

For a binary DGP, “explosion” means saturation. Probabilities race toward 0 or 1. After a handful of large effects, the outcome is no longer a noisy, resistant human outcome. It is almost determined.

The same is true on bounded scales. If controls are normally distributed with mean 0 and SD 1, then $P(Y > 1)$ is 15.9% and $P(Y > 2)$ is 2.3%. After a $d = 0.8$ shift those become 42.1% and 11.5%. A large effect does not merely move the average. It turns rare states into common states. If thousands of ordinary interventions did that, everyday distributions would visibly lurch toward ceilings and floors whenever ordinary inputs changed.

And the large-effect claim does not get rescued by heterogeneity. If only fraction $h$ of people are true responders and everyone else has zero effect, then an average $d = 0.8$ requires a responder-only shift of roughly $0.8 / h$:

| Responding fraction | Responder-only effect needed for average $d = 0.8$ |
| ---: | ---: |
| 100% | 0.8 |
| 50% | 1.6 |
| 20% | 4.0 |
| 10% | 8.0 |

If the effect is broad, it consumes the population variance budget. If it is narrow, then responders have to move by multiple standard deviations. Either way, it should be obvious.

The small-$N$ publication boundary explains why the literature can look this way without that world existing. For a balanced two-group design,

$$
s_{\hat d} \approx \frac{2}{\sqrt{N}}, \qquad |\hat d| \gtrsim \frac{3.92}{\sqrt{N}} \text{ for two-sided } p < 0.05.
$$

That makes the significance rule itself an effect-size filter. At total $N = 20$, the minimum publishable $|\hat d|$ is about $0.88$. At $N = 40$, it is about $0.62$. At $N = 80$, about $0.44$. At $N = 200$, about $0.28$. At $N = 400$, about $0.20$. At small $N$, “statistically significant” already means “observed effect size large enough to look exciting.”

This is why three different empirical literatures keep converging on the same correction. Szucs and Ioannidis found that published cognitive-neuroscience and psychology papers were packed with large nominal effects and low power [@szucs2017]. Pereira, Horwitz, and Ioannidis found that very large medical effects usually came from small first trials and shrank sharply as more evidence accumulated [@pereira2012largeeffects]. Registered Reports cut the positive-result rate dramatically once publication decisions were made before the data were known [@scheel2021], and the antidepressant-trial record shows the same thing from the registry side: the published literature looked much more positive than the underlying FDA file [@turner2008]. The point is not that one field cheated more than another. The point is that small samples plus a significance filter manufacture a literature full of apparently large, apparently decisive effects.

That life is one in which your fate is dictated by trivia, most of it outside your control. A warm cup, a stray smell, an overhead bulb --- each a lever big enough to swing trust, honesty, testosterone, or the odds of a heart attack. Keeping a marriage, a job, or a working cardiovascular system would be a daily gauntlet: meticulous planning to control the inputs, and persistent luck when you couldn't. No equilibrium you built over years of practice, effort, or cooperation would be stable, because any small thing could knock it over. Choosing the wrong shirt could cost you the promotion. Sitting in the wrong chair could cost you the deal. The right cup of coffee could save your marriage. The wrong one could end it.

And if every small thing mattered, then in another sense nothing would. You couldn't decide what was worth your attention, because the list of things with life-sized consequences would be infinite. You couldn't learn from experience, because each day's outcome would be dominated by a different lurking confounder. You couldn't give advice to your children, because whatever rule you landed on would be overturned by the next trivial intervention to come down the pike. A world in which everything is a huge treatment effect is a world in which agency, planning, and accumulated knowledge collapse into noise.

So the nightmare world is a reductio. If the published small-$N$ effect-size distribution were literal, reality would be packed with hundreds of thousands to millions of large, easy, high-variance causal levers. Ordinary DGPs cannot absorb that. They either inflate in variance, saturate at boundaries, collapse probabilities toward 0 or 1, or reveal that the supposedly independent effects are really the same few mechanisms wearing different names. The sane alternative is the boring one: most published small-$N$ large effects are not real large effects. We are lucky the literature is mostly not true.

## How This Weirdly Unscientific Culture Became Normal {#how-this-weirdly-unscientific-culture-became-normal}

Humans keep inventing this worldview. In every era, in every culture, some version of it shows up: your fate is dictated by forces outside your control --- the stars, the gods, karma, ancestors, trauma, your microbiome, your vibration --- and also every small thing you do is loaded with ultimate consequence [@npr2022astrology]. You are simultaneously helpless and supernaturally responsible, and the contradiction is the point; a doctrine that resolved cleanly into either fatalism or agency would lose half its customers. The meme is stable because it pays two constituencies at once. For the individual it is the most flattering theory of the self ever invented: when things go well, you worked the system; when things go badly, the system (or karma [@lerner1980just], or someone else's negative energy) worked you. Vague enough personality claims feel personally true to almost everyone [@forer1949]. For predatory institutions the same structure is a revenue model, because if every small thing matters and the mechanisms are invisible, the list of fixes someone can sell you is infinite. Religions sold indulgences on this logic for centuries [@bossy1985christianity]; prosperity preachers sell seed-faith donations on it now [@bowler2013blessed]; a \$6.8-trillion global wellness industry sells supplements, cleanses, and energy work [@gwi2023wellness]; MLM uplines sell the dream that the right mindset produces the right income, and most participants make little or no money doing it [@ftc2018mlm]. The product varies; the sales pitch is identical --- *there is a hidden lever, you have been pulling it wrong, and I can show you how.* The pitch only works in a world of huge treatment effects. In a world of small, noisy, hard-won effects, no one needs a guru.

The published scientific literature has its own version of the same meme. A graduate student runs 42 undergraduates through a two-minute posture manipulation and reports that the treatment raised testosterone by 30% and risk tolerance by 45%. A lab runs 67 undergraduates past a plate of radishes and reports that willpower is a depletable resource that cookies consume. A paper runs 30 students through a scrambled-sentence prime and reports that reading words about old age makes people walk 15% slower down a hallway. Each study has a tiny sample, a tiny intervention, and a huge reported effect. Each was published in a prestigious journal. Each produced a decade of follow-up research, popular-press coverage, TED talks, management consulting, and policy recommendations. The structure is the same as the religious version: a small, nearly invisible lever produces an enormous, life-sized consequence. The statistics are different from magic, but the shape of the claim is not.

### What a flat prior commits you to

Strictly speaking, NHST does not have a prior. That is part of the problem. A *p*-value is not (P(H_0 \| data)), and a confidence interval is not born as a posterior distribution over the true effect. But when readers convert a significant estimate and its confidence interval into belief about the true effect, the object they are tacitly using is the flat-prior posterior in disguise. In the normal approximation, δ̂ \| δ ∼ *N*(δ, *s*²); combine that likelihood with the improper prior π(δ) ∝ 1 and the posterior is δ \| δ̂ ∼ *N*(δ̂, *s*²) --- the same Gaussian bell that the confidence distribution traces. That is the no-shrinkage prior: not a probability distribution, not normalizable, and perfectly happy to assign equal density to equal-width intervals around *d* = 0.2, *d* = 5, and *d* = 1,000. No one believes this out loud. **@fig-prior-comparison** draws what the belief would look like. The publication system behaves as if authors and reviewers were doing exactly that calculation.

NHST contains no prior at all. The flat prior appears when readers read a significant estimate and its confidence interval as a belief distribution over the true effect---which is how unshrunk point estimates routinely get read. With that implicit flat prior, a significant *p*-value at *n* = 30 is treated as evidence for an effect. A reasonable prior --- one that puts, say, 95% of its mass in \|*d*\| \< 0.7, because that's where psychology-sized effects actually live --- would demand far more data before allowing the same conclusion. The flat prior removes this brake, and the consequence is the gamification of discovery: instead of asking "how much data do I need to detect a psychologically plausible effect," a researcher asks "how little data can I get away with and still clear the threshold." The answer is often remarkably little --- thirty subjects, if the effect you report is big enough. Coupled with the publication filter, where journals print the papers that clear *p* \< 0.05 and not the ones that don't, you end up with a literature that consists almost entirely of studies that had to report implausibly large effects in order to exist, and almost none that reported the actual small effects that were there. The published record contains the untrue things and excludes the true ones. @fig-publication-boundary and @fig-prior-comparison together are the mechanism. @fig-published-d-distribution is what the mechanism produces.

![The current published-original candidate corpus as a distribution of paper-level effect sizes. 77% of papers have median D >= 0.2, 42% have median D >= 0.5, and 22% have median D >= 0.8.](_generated/figures/figure5_published_d_distribution.png){#fig-published-d-distribution}

Once that worldview is culturally normal, the rest of the machinery is easy to justify: tiny studies, huge estimates, confident prose, and a literature calibrated to discovery theater rather than measurement.

## Objections

*Four objections are worth taking seriously. None of them saves the literature this essay is about.*

### "But there are exceptions where effects really are large." {#obj-exceptions}

Appendix B catalogs the exceptions in detail. The taxonomy is not alphabetical and not by academic field; it is by architectural type. Infectious disease control. Deficiency replacement. Mechanical and structural interventions. A handful of cardiovascular and metabolic pharmacologic classes. A short list of psychiatric interventions with clear mechanistic targets. A few educational interventions that happen to combine intensity, targeting, and a weak counterfactual. Behavioral policy interventions that change defaults rather than minds. Agricultural and environmental interventions that address a genuine limiting factor.

What these share is the shape of the compound model with each factor pushed favorably. A dominant causal bottleneck means *k* is effectively 1, so 1/√*k* is not small. A short causal chain means the product of path coefficients is close to 1. A large counterfactual gap means the control arm's shared-ingredient fraction is close to 0. A well-measured proximal outcome means *r~xx~* is close to 1. When all four structural factors are favorable and the intervention is tested rigorously enough that the selection filter does no work, the effect can be enormous. Antibiotics against susceptible pathogens produce *d*-equivalents that do not fit on normal charts. Insulin moved life expectancy in Type 1 diabetes from months to decades. Cataract surgery restores sight.

The pattern of exceptions is the strongest possible confirmation of the rule. Large effects require specific structural conditions. Those conditions are rare. Their rarity is why most effects are small. Interventions that meet the full checklist are not distributed uniformly across human problems; they cluster in a few therapeutic areas (infection, deficiency, mechanical dysfunction) and a few behavioral domains (default architecture, choice of instruction intensity). Outside those clusters, the expected effect of any intervention is small, and researchers who report large effects should be read as having either (a) found a rare genuine exception that satisfies the checklist, or (b) produced an estimate that will not survive contact with replication, scale-up, or bias correction.

### "It's just p-hacking. Fix the filter and the rest takes care of itself." {#obj-phacking}

Fix every form of p-hacking tomorrow. A two-arm behavioral study with *N* = 40 still crosses *p* \< 0.05 on an observed *d* of roughly 0.62, still has 23% power against a true *d* = 0.4, still selects upper-tail draws from its sampling distribution. Type M inflation on a small-*N* significant finding is approximately 2× under fully honest analysis---a property of the sampling distribution, not of researcher behavior.

Registered Reports make this concrete. Scheel, Schijen, and Lakens [@scheel2021] compared 71 preregistered Registered Reports to 152 standard articles and found positive-result rates of 96% versus 44%---a 52 percentage-point drop once p-hacking is removed by design. But 44% of honestly-analyzed studies still cross significance, in a world where most true effects are under *d* = 0.25. Those positive findings are inflated upper-tail draws. They still don't replicate cleanly.

The filter is not the primary disease. The primary disease is that the resolution of the instrument doesn't match the size of the object being measured. Remove p-hacking and you trade a literature of inflated positives for a literature of honest small-sample noise plus the rare large effect. Cleaner, but not informative.

### "Researchers calibrate sample size to the true effect." {#obj-calibration}

This is the anthropic principle: investigators have priors about effect sizes, run power analyses, and size their *N* accordingly. A published *N* = 40 reflects an investigator who had reason to expect a detectable effect at that sample.

The best version of this objection is van Zwet, Gelman, and Więcek [@vanzwet2026], whose model of 80,000 studies across 11 curated corpora includes exactly this claim. Their data refute it.

Across their corpora---Cochrane, clinical trials, ecology, political science, exercise research, economics, psychology, psychotherapy, education, intelligence---**3--11% of studies achieve 80% probability of significance**. Not eighty percent of studies. Three to eleven. Political science: 7%. Cochrane: 10%. Exercise and cognition: 3%. The modal published study is radically underpowered for the effect it is nominally detecting. vZGW's own interpretation is that researchers size studies "just about large enough to assess the direction of the effect of interest, but not larger"---under pressures of time, money, and logistics.

That is not calibration. It is the minimum sample required to cross the filter on a favorable noise draw, which is exactly what the rest of this essay has argued. The anthropic principle is real; it does not say what the objection needs it to say. It says researchers size for filter-crossing, not for detection.

### "At least the sign is right." {#obj-sign}

The sharpest version of this objection is vZGW [@vanzwet2026]. Their model reports that published significant findings carry the correct sign 82--96% of the time at \|*z*\| = 1.96 and 94--99% at \|*z*\| ≥ 1.96, across all 11 of their curated corpora. Their interpretation: *"empirical research often leads to true findings---in the sense of correctly identifying the direction of an effect---that do not replicate in the sense of achieving statistically significant result in the same direction as the original study."* Magnitudes are inflated; direction is informative; the replication crisis is overstated.

This is wrong, and the way it is wrong is instructive.

**The numbers are not a measurement.** They are the output of a model. vZGW fit a mixture of half-normals to the observed \|*z*\| distribution in each corpus, apply a Hedges single-parameter correction for publication bias, deconvolve out a standard normal noise component, and read off a posterior distribution over the true SNR. The 82--96% correct-sign figure is what that posterior implies under the model's assumptions. It is not the result of comparing published signs to any external ground truth.

**The model's central assumption fails in exactly the subfields where the crisis lives.** The assumption is that observed *z* equals true SNR plus standard normal noise. For this to hold, the published estimate must be an unbiased draw from its sampling distribution. That assumption holds when the study is well-identified: the construct is measured cleanly, the intervention is delivered as described, selection into the sample is exogenous, the analysis is fixed in advance. In that regime, upper-tail draws that cross the filter are disproportionately sign-concordant with the true effect, because the true mean and forward-direction noise point the same way. Correct sign 90% of the time falls out.

The assumption does not hold when the research process itself has degrees of freedom correlated with direction---specification selection, construct misspecification, demand characteristics, identification strategies that don't identify what they claim. In that regime, the "noise" is not symmetric around the true effect. It is drawn from the space of specifications the researcher was willing to report. The filter still selects upper-tail draws, but those draws align with **what the researcher expected to find**, not with reality. A field that operates on a wrong shared prior produces a literature full of findings pointing in the theory-predicted direction, regardless of whether the theory is correct. vZGW's model reads the resulting internal consistency as "high correct-sign rate." It is not. It is internal consistency with a wrong prior.

**The direct empirical test defeats the model where it matters.** vZGW's model does not have access to the true sign of the effect. Large preregistered replications do, at least approximately, because they restore the conditions under which observed *z* really is an unbiased draw. Run the test:

-   Chabris et al. 2012 [@chabris2012]: 12 previously-published candidate-IQ-gene effects tested in roughly 10,000 subjects. Replicated: **zero**. A coin produces six. Published sign correct rate: 0%.
-   Duncan and Keller 2011 [@duncan2011]: 103 novel psychiatric G×E findings, 10 direct replications, 27% replication rate, pair-level sign match near 50%. Coin-equivalent.
-   The Registered Replication Report program and Many Labs 3: ego depletion, facial feedback, professor priming, elderly priming, warmth priming---in aggregate, sign concordance with originals in the 40--60% range.

In the subfields where the replication crisis actually landed---candidate genes, social priming, much of classical social psychology, the first decade of behavioral genetics---the empirical sign concordance between original small-*N* findings and large-*N* replications is at or below chance. vZGW's model predicts 82--96%. Reality delivers 0--50%.

**This is not a "different subfield" problem.** It is a model-specification problem. vZGW's framework cannot distinguish a literature that gets the sign right 90% of the time from a literature that is internally consistent 90% of the time in the direction of a wrong theory. Their model assumes the first; their data are compatible with either; the fields where the assumption is testable show the second. Their optimism is what their model outputs when their assumption is satisfied. In the fields where that optimism would actually matter, the assumption fails.

The replication crisis is not about magnitudes being imprecise while directions remain true. It is about the research process in specific subfields producing published literatures that do not carry reliable information about reality---not about magnitude, not about sign. vZGW have described a mathematical property of their model. They have not described the literature.

## Appendix F --- Why most effects must be small

Start with a contradiction. Suppose every intervention had a large effect. Every vitamin, every parenting choice, every policy, every supplement, every app notification, every teacher, every workout, every therapeutic technique, every political ad, every workplace intervention---every one of them moved the outcome by a full standard deviation. That world cannot exist. It cannot exist because the outcome you are moving---your cholesterol, your child's test score, your depression rating, the country's vote share---only varies by so much. There is a finite amount of standard deviation to be moved. If one thing moves it by a full SD, most other things can move it by almost nothing, because the variance budget is already spent. You can have a few big effects or many small ones; you cannot have many big ones. This is arithmetic, not psychology.

Real life is a standing refutation of the large-effects world. If every new drug had a large effect, pharmaceutical R&D would not spend a billion dollars per approval to find one that works [@scannell2012eroom]. If every teaching method had a large effect, there would be no education gap. If every diet had a large effect, obesity would be trivially curable. If every therapy had a large effect, mental illness would be rare.

Scale it up. If every nudge worked at the pilot-study scale, behavioral economics would have solved climate change, voter turnout, and retirement saving by lunch. If every published psychology finding reproduced at its published magnitude, a forty-person undergraduate study that reported a Cohen's *d* of 0.8 from a two-minute intervention would represent a manipulation of humans as powerful as a strong drug. The literature as published describes a world in which nothing is hard: no diseases resist treatment, no students resist teaching, no behaviors resist nudging, no habits resist self-help. That world does not resemble any actual country, classroom, clinic, or household. The outcomes behavioral and biomedical science measure are, by definition, the ones that resist easy movement. That is *why* they are the ones being studied.

The rest of this section makes the argument precise in three layers, then shows that measurements confirm it.

*The first layer is about the outcomes themselves.* Why do mood, weight, test scores, and blood pressure have finite, predictable variance in the first place? And why do the outcomes that *don't* --- earthquakes, financial crashes, viral cascades --- permit single interventions to produce very large effects?

*The second layer is about the causes.* Given a bounded outcome, the mechanisms that move it have to be small. Five bounds show how small and why.

*The third layer is about the research ecosystem.* Published studies pass through a significance filter that inflates what survives, a demand structure that contaminates every lab experiment, and a set of scaling frictions that keep pilots from reproducing. Eleven mechanisms in all; six admit exact derivations. Three independent empirical literatures then confirm the total size of the resulting gap.

### Why outcomes are bounded at all {#why-outcomes-are-bounded}

Before we get to estimation---before Gelman on selection, Pearl on mediation, or Spearman on reliability---there is a prior question. Why is there a stable, measurable "effect of *X* on *Y*" at all? Why does shoving a complex system produce a reproducible, finite displacement rather than chaos, runaway, or nothing? The answer is not a fact about statistics; it is a fact about the systems we study. Five properties rule out the world of arbitrarily large effects, and each of them can be seen through a thought experiment: imagine the property didn't hold, look at what reality would have to look like, and notice that it doesn't.

*Outcomes are aggregates.* Blood pressure, test score, depression rating, vote share, GDP, body weight---every outcome behavioral and biomedical science measures is a sum or average over enormous numbers of underlying micro-events. The central limit theorem then squeezes the distribution of any such aggregate toward a bell with finite variance. *Suppose this weren't true.* Then a single neuron firing or a single cell dividing could shift the whole outcome; your blood pressure this morning would be uncorrelated with your blood pressure yesterday; the class average on the retest would have no systematic relationship to the class average on the first test. Talking about "the mean" of anything would be nonsense, because the next measurement would wander anywhere. We know outcomes don't behave this way because *re-measuring* the same person or the same group gives a similar number. The aggregation that makes re-measurement meaningful also fixes the variance and caps what any single input can do.

*Systems dissipate.* Biological, social, and mechanical systems lose energy to friction, noise, and entropy; perturbations decay, usually exponentially, with a timescale set by the system's own structure. *Suppose they didn't.* Then every cup of coffee you've ever drunk would still be moving your heart rate right now. Every argument you've ever had would still be raising your cortisol. Every warm summer day from your childhood would still be contributing to your current body temperature. Every teacher you had in third grade would still be adding a discrete amount to your SAT. The effect of a one-shot intervention would sum forever. It obviously doesn't. You wake up in the morning roughly the same person you were yesterday, which means the system has quietly eaten almost everything that happened to it. The machinery that erases your personal history also erases most of the effect of a short-duration study intervention.

*Homeostatic feedback.* On top of passive dissipation, living and social systems maintain active negative-feedback loops around set points [@cannon1932homeostasis]. Body temperature, blood glucose, cortisol, clearing prices, wage levels, political equilibria---each is held in a narrow range by regulatory machinery that detects deviation and counteracts it. *Suppose there were no homeostasis.* The first time you skipped a meal, your blood glucose would have crashed to zero. The first cold day would have dropped your body temperature to ambient. The first bad week at work would have permanently lowered your mood by a standard deviation. A single missed dose of medication would not merely be suboptimal; it would be lethal. You are reading this sentence because your body and the society you live in are dense with restoring forces that undo most perturbations. Interventions have to overpower those forces. Most don't.

*Substrates are finite.* A child has twenty-four hours in a day. A cell has finite ATP and membrane surface. A household has finite income. A country has finite tax base. Effects on an outcome are built out of the same substrate the outcome itself depends on, so the intervention cannot move the outcome by more than the substrate allows. *Suppose substrates were unbounded.* Then an extra hour of tutoring wouldn't come out of something else the child was doing; you could add hours to the day until test scores hit any number you wanted. You could stack antidepressants until depression scores were arbitrarily negative. You could pile on exercise until resting heart rate was zero. Conservation says otherwise. Every intervention competes against the interventions already present, against the physical limits of the outcome scale, and against whatever else the substrate was being used for.

*Equilibria absorb repeated interventions.* Wherever agents interact repeatedly --- markets, workplaces, classrooms, parliaments, households, doctor's offices --- the per-interaction effect has to be small, or the system would have moved to a corner and stayed there long ago. *Suppose each interaction had a large effect.* Then the first company to run a persuasive ad would own the market by the next quarter [@fama1970efficient]. The first teacher who hit on a good lesson would have raised their students' lifetime earnings by orders of magnitude above their peers. A single therapy session would cure or entrench any condition. A single argument would resolve or destroy any marriage. Every supposedly "viral" idea would actually go viral; politics would lurch from consensus to consensus each news cycle; stock prices would move to zero or infinity and stop.

Instead, these systems are visibly stable. Their parameters drift on timescales of years, not minutes, because every large shove gets met by countervailing shoves. Competitors copy the good pitch. Substitutes appear for the taxed cigarette. Counter-arguments form against the persuasive ad. Attention moves on from the viral post. Prices reprice. Per-interaction effects that look large under the microscope of a single study equilibrate against everyone else's effects, and what the system settles into is close to what it was before. Most outcomes worth studying are embedded in such systems, which is why any pilot-scale effect decays as the intervention moves from the researcher's lab into a world of adapting agents.

*The exceptions prove the rule.* Some outcomes *do* have effectively unbounded variance: earthquake magnitudes, financial crashes, pandemic cases, viral cascades, wealth, war deaths [@clauset2009powerlaw; @taleb2007blackswan; @gutenberg1944earthquake]. These are outcomes where a single patient zero produces millions of cases, one bank failure cascades into a global crash, one post goes viral, one weapon causes a war. Each of the first five properties breaks down for them. A single event determines the outcome (no averaging). No restoring force intervenes (no dissipation, no homeostasis). Shocks propagate through long-range dependence (no aggregation bound). The substrate is effectively unbounded over relevant horizons. That is the world in which single-study frequentist analysis is most dangerous, because the regularity assumptions it relies on are exactly the properties such outcomes violate. The rest of science --- the part that studies mood, attitudes, test scores, behavior, physiology, prices --- does not live in that world. Its outcomes are bounded by one or more of the five constraints, which is why its effects are small.

The outcomes behavioral and biomedical science actually measure sit inside all five of these constraints. Given that, the only remaining question is how small a cause acting on one of them has to be, which is what the eleven mechanisms below answer. Keeping the two layers distinct matters for reading the literature. A large effect on a power-law outcome (earthquakes, pandemic cases, viral cascades) is physically possible. A large effect on mood, attitudes, test scores, physiology, or behavior is not.

### Why mechanisms acting on those outcomes have to be small {#why-mechanisms-are-bounded}

Given a bounded outcome, consider what a large mechanism would mean. If a single gene determined adult height, genetics would be a one-page paper. If a single teacher determined adult income, the achievement gap would track teacher rosters. If a single childhood experience determined personality, siblings would be identical. If a single conversation determined an attitude, no one would ever change their mind twice. None of these worlds exist. The mechanisms that actually move outcomes have to share a variance budget with thousands of others doing the same thing, and they do it through an estimator whose view of them is itself bounded.

Five mechanisms across two families make this precise. The first family is the variance budget itself: how much any single cause is permitted to claim when the outcome is produced by many of them, either in parallel or along a chain. The second family is what the estimator can physically see: the contrast between treatment and control, the noise added by measurement, the ceiling imposed by bounded scales. All five hold regardless of who runs the study or how carefully. Four of the five admit exact derivations collected at the end of this section.

#### Constraints on the effect-size budget {#group-budget .mech-group}

If an outcome has finite variance and is produced by many causes, those causes have to share the variance budget---so most of them have to be small. The same constraint applied along a causal chain instead of across parallel causes gives geometric decay in chain length. You cannot escape both at once: if the fan-in is narrow, the chain is usually long, and vice versa. These are the most physical of the eleven mechanisms in the sense that they hold regardless of who does the study or how it is run.

##### Polygenicity and the piranha bound {#mech-polygenicity}

The variance budget for a bounded-variance outcome forces many-cause effects toward zero.

When a bounded-variance outcome is produced by *k* independent causes collectively explaining a share *h*² of the variance, the variance budget forces the average standardized effect to scale as *O*(*k*^−1/2^). You cannot pack many large, independent causes into the same bounded-variance outcome; they eat each other. For the outcome model

::: math-display
*Y* = Σ~*i*~ β~*i*~ *X~i~* + ε
:::

with standardized mutually uncorrelated *X~i~* and Var(*Y*) = 1, we have Σ β~*i*~² ≤ *h*² ≤ 1. Under equipartition β~*i*~² = *h*²/*k*, so the per-input standardized effect is \|β~*i*~\| = (*h*²/*k*)^1/2^ and the per-input Cohen's *d* is roughly 2β~*i*~. The piranha theorem (Tosh et al. 2025) sharpens this: for unit-variance predictors *X₁, ..., X_p* and outcome *Y*,

::: math-display
Σ~*i*~ Corr(*X~i~*, *Y*)² ≤ 1 + 2 Σ~*i* \< *j*~ \|Corr(*X~i~*, *X~j~*)\|.
:::

If *p* predictors each claim correlation ≥ ρ with *Y*, either ρ ≲ 1/√*p* or the predictors are substantially mutually correlated. The "many large, independent effects" world is formally excluded.

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG A.** Polygenic fan-in. The unit-variance constraint on *Y* forces Σ βᵢ² ≤ 1, so that under any near-equipartition of causal contributions the typical \|βᵢ\| is of order *k*^−1/2^. Large independent effects "eat" the variance budget.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.* Adult height has *h*² ≈ 0.8 and Yengo et al. 2022 identified roughly 12,111 independent genome-wide-significant SNPs jointly explaining 40--45% of variance: the mean per-SNP *R*² is about 3 × 10^−5^, giving per-SNP \|β\| ≈ 0.006 and per-SNP *d* ≈ 0.01. Educational attainment (Lee et al. 2018) finds 1,271 lead SNPs with PGI *R*² ≈ 11--13%. Chabris et al. 2012 tested 12 candidate IQ genes in ∼10,000 subjects and replicated essentially none --- the pre-GWAS candidate-gene era operated with effect-size priors off by two orders of magnitude. Brain-behavior associations collapse from *r* = 0.2--0.8 in small samples to *r* = 0.01 in adequately powered ones (Marek et al. 2022).

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** For a socially manipulable trait with *h*² ≈ 0.10--0.20 spread across *k* = 50--200 contributing mechanisms, the per-cause \|*d*\| is bounded at roughly **0.05--0.15**. Szaszi, Higney & Gelman 2022 put the observed post-correction nudge average at *d* ≈ 0.08 --- within a factor of two of the polygenicity prediction.
:::

Tosh, Greengard, Goodrich, Gelman, Vehtari & Hsu 2025  [@tosh2024], *Notices of the AMS*; Boyle, Li & Pritchard 2017  [@boyle2017omnigenic], *Cell* 169(7):1177--1186; Chabris, Lee, Cesarini, Benjamin & Laibson 2015  [@chabris2015fourth], *Current Directions in Psychological Science* 24(4):304--312; Turkheimer 2000  [@turkheimer2000], *Current Directions in Psychological Science* 9(5):160--164; Yengo et al. 2022  [@yengo2022], *Nature* 610(7933):704--712; Marek et al. 2022  [@marek2022], *Nature* 603(7902):654--660.

##### Mediation chains and geometric attenuation {#mech-mediation}

Behavioral interventions travel through chains of intermediate steps; the total standardized effect is the product of the per-link path coefficients.

Treatments rarely act directly on the distal outcome of interest. They act on a proximal mediator, which influences a second mediator, which influences a third, which eventually influences the outcome. Under linearity the total standardized effect of the whole chain is the product of the per-link standardized path coefficients, and because each \|β~ℓ~\| ≤ 1 (Cauchy--Schwarz on unit-variance variables), the product decays geometrically with chain length. Most famous behavioral interventions are implicitly 3- or 4-hop chains, and the algebra tells you medium-sized distal effects are nearly impossible.

::: math-display
δ~distal~ = ∏~ℓ = 1..*n*~ β~ℓ~,   \|*d*~distal~\| ≈ 2 · \|∏ β~ℓ~\|
:::

Pearl 2001's natural indirect effect collapses to exactly β₁ β₂ in the linear no-interaction case. Composition with classical measurement error adds reliability attenuation as an additional multiplicative factor ∏ √*r~ℓ~*. Fritz & MacKinnon 2007 showed that detecting the indirect effect *ab* with each path equal to a "small" 0.14 requires *N* ≈ 20,886 for 80% power --- a quantitative proof that composed small effects are operationally invisible in typical samples.

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG B.** Four-link mediation chain. Under linearity, total standardized effect = β₁ β₂ β₃ β₄. With per-link coefficients between 0.3 and 0.4, the total shrinks below the "small" threshold.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.*

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 42. Mediation chains and geometric attenuation**

| per-link β | 4-link product | implied d | N /arm at 80% |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0.2 | 0.0016 | 0.003 | 1,600,000 |
| 0.3 | 0.0081 | 0.016 | 62,500 |
| 0.4 | 0.0256 | 0.051 | 6,200 |
| 0.5 | 0.0625 | 0.125 | 1,000 |
| 0.7 | 0.2401 | 0.495 | 65 |
:::

Only when every link exceeds 0.5 do distal *d*\'s enter Cohen\'s "medium" range. A typical behavioral example: nudge → attention (β₁ = 0.30) → intention (β₂ = 0.35) → short-term behavior (β₃ = 0.40; Webb & Sheeran intention--behavior meta-analysis) → 6-month outcome (β₄ = 0.30). Product:  0.0126, implying *d* ≈ 0.025 and an 80%-power sample of *N* ≈ 25,000/arm.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** With realistic per-link coefficients in \[0.3, 0.4\] and chain length *n* = 3--4, the mediation bound gives **\|*d*~distal~\| ≲ 0.02--0.13** --- almost always below the *d* = 0.2 "small" threshold.
:::

Pearl 2001  [@pearl2001mediation], *Proceedings of UAI-01*, 411--420; MacKinnon, Lockwood, Hoffman, West & Sheets 2002  [@mackinnon2002mediation], *Psychological Methods* 7(1):83--104; Loken & Gelman 2017  [@loken2017measurement], *Science* 355(6325):584--585.

#### What the estimator can actually see {#group-estimator .mech-group}

A study does not measure an effect; it measures a contrast between an arm that got the treatment and an arm that did not, and if the control arm already has most of what the treatment adds, the contrast is small. Every instrument adds measurement noise that attenuates the underlying signal. And every bounded outcome --- accuracy near 100%, mortality near 0% --- imposes a hard ceiling on how far the treatment mean can move. None of these depend on journals or researchers. They depend on what you can physically see from the outside of a stochastic system.

##### The counterfactual gap against active controls {#mech-counterfactual}

Effect sizes are properties of contrasts, not of treatments; the better the control, the smaller the gap.

The estimand τ = *E*\[*Y*(1) − *Y*(0)\] is a difference, and its magnitude depends on *both* arms. In mature domains the control arm receives most of the causally active ingredients --- standard care, an existing teacher, a baseline reminder --- so the contrast shrinks by subtraction. Write δ~T~ = μ~T~ − μ₀ and δ~C~ = μ~C~ − μ₀ for the treatment and control arms against a hypothetical no-intervention baseline μ₀:

::: math-display
*d*~*T*\ vs\ *C*~ = (δ~T~ − δ~C~) / σ ≤ *d*~*T*\ vs\ 0~ · (1 − ρ),  ρ = δ~C~ / δ~T~.
:::

In the Hernán--Robins DAG with a shared mechanism *S* activated by both arms and specific mechanisms δ~T~, δ~C~, the active-control contrast mechanically cancels the shared component.

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG C.** Active-control DAG. Both arms *T* and *C* share the mechanism *S* (expectancy, monitoring, clinician contact). The observed contrast (*T* vs *C*) cancels *S*\'s contribution; only the difference of the specific effects δ~T~ − δ~C~ survives.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.* DellaVigna & Linos 2022 (*Econometrica* 90(1):81--116): 126 RCTs at two US Nudge Units covering 23 million individuals, compared against the published academic nudge literature. Academic mean: **8.7 pp take-up increase** (33.4% over control). Nudge-unit mean: **1.4 pp** (8.0% over control). A 6.2× gap, of which DellaVigna and Linos attribute roughly 70% to publication selection under low power and the remainder to intervention-characteristic differences. Kraft 2020 shows that only ∼13% of ∼2,000 education RCT effect sizes on standardized achievement exceed *d* = 0.40; in his 2023 follow-up, 36% are below *d* = 0.05. Cipriani et al. 2018 (*Lancet*): antidepressant drug-vs-placebo SMDs ≈ 0.30, drug-vs-drug contrasts cluster near zero.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** For light-touch policy interventions against realistic active counterfactuals, ***d* ≲ 0.05--0.10**. Kraft's revised education-RCT benchmarks put "large" at *d* ≥ 0.20, not Cohen's 0.80.
:::

Freedman 1987  [@freedman1987equipoise], *NEJM* 317(3):141--145; DellaVigna & Linos 2022  [@dellavigna2022], *Econometrica* 90(1):81--116; Kraft 2020  [@kraft2020effectsize], *Educational Researcher* 49(4):241--253; Cipriani et al. 2018  [@cipriani2018], *Lancet* 391(10128):1357--1366.

##### The signal-to-noise ceiling from measurement {#mech-measurement}

Observed correlations are bounded by the reliabilities of the measurements that produced them --- Spearman's 1904 theorem, still binding.

Every observed correlation or standardized mean difference is bounded above by the reliabilities of the measures that produced it. With *X* = *X\** + *e~X~* and *Y* = *Y\** + *e~Y~* (independent errors), reliability *r~XX~* = Var(*X\**)/Var(*X*):

::: math-display
Corr(*X*, *Y*) = Corr(*X\**, *Y\**) · √(*r~XX~* *r~YY~*),   *d*~obs~ = *d*~true~ · √*r~YY~*.
:::

Loken & Gelman 2017 add the selection-interaction point: under conditioning on significance in small noisy samples, mean attenuation coexists with upward inflation of surviving estimates. Noise damages truth-telling bidirectionally.

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG D.** Classical measurement-error structure. Latent causes *X\**, *Y\** are contaminated by independent errors *U_X*, *U_Y* to produce observed *X*, *Y*. Spearman attenuation means the observed correlation is bounded by √(*r~XX~* *r~YY~*) times the true correlation.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.* Hedge, Powell & Sumner 2018 report test-retest ICCs for seven "robust" cognitive tasks: **Stroop RT-cost 0.60--0.66, Flanker RT-cost 0.40, Posner/Navon effects near 0, go/no-go commission errors 0.76, SSRT ≈ 0.43**. Elliott et al. 2020 meta-analyzed task-fMRI across 90 experiments (*N* = 1,008): mean ICC **0.397**; 56% of values fall in the "poor" range below 0.40; only 20% reach "good/excellent." With a brain measure at ICC 0.40 and a questionnaire at 0.80, the observed-correlation ceiling is √(0.40 · 0.80) ≈ 0.57 regardless of the underlying truth. The *reliability paradox* (Hedge et al.): the very property that makes Stroop and flanker robust at the group level --- tiny between-subject variance --- is what destroys their power for individual differences. The tasks used most often to operationalize cognitive control are the tasks worst suited to it.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** For self-report outcomes (α ≈ 0.8), *d*~obs~ ≤ 0.89 *d*~true~; for IAT (≈ 0.5), ≤ 0.71; for flanker (≈ 0.4), ≤ 0.63; for task-fMRI correlated with a 0.4-reliable behavioral measure, the correlation ceiling is **0.40**, not 1.0. No amount of *N* fixes this --- additional data pushes you toward the attenuated limit, not past it.
:::

Spearman 1904  [@spearman1904], *American Journal of Psychology* 15(1):72--101; Hedge, Powell & Sumner 2018  [@hedge2018reliability], *Behavior Research Methods* 50(3):1166--1186; Elliott et al. 2020  [@elliott2020fmri], *Psychological Science* 31(7):792--806; Loken & Gelman 2017  [@loken2017measurement], *Science* 355(6325):584--585.

##### Ceiling and floor effects on bounded outcomes {#mech-ceiling}

If the outcome can't go higher, the effect size can't go higher.

When the outcome is bounded --- a proportion in \[0, 1\], a Likert score in \[1, 7\], accuracy on an easy task, a reaction time above a physiological floor --- the space of possible post-treatment means is mathematically compressed. A high baseline leaves no room for improvement, and under truncation the SD itself shrinks, so *d* = Δμ/σ is doubly bounded. For a dichotomous outcome with baseline *p*₀, Cohen's *h*-statistic upper bound is

::: math-display
*h*~max~(*p*₀) = π − 2 · arcsin(√*p*₀).
:::

For a bounded continuous outcome with maximum *M* and baseline mean μ₀, the Bhatia--Davis inequality gives σ ≤ √(μ₀ (*M* − μ₀)).

*Concrete numbers.*

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 43. Ceiling and floor effects on bounded outcomes**

| baseline p ₀ | h max |
| --- | --- |
| 0.30 | ≈1.98 |
| 0.50 | ≈1.57 |
| 0.70 | ≈1.16 |
| 0.90 | ≈0.64 |
| 0.95 | ≈0.45 |
| 0.99 | ≈0.28 |
:::

At *p*₀ = 0.95, the physically maximum improvement (*p*₁ → 1) produces *h* ≈ 0.45 --- categorically below Cohen's "large." Halving mortality from 2% to 1% (a life saved per 100, an immense absolute gain) produces *h* ≈ 0.08 --- "less than small." The WAIS-IV caps at FSIQ ≈ 160, so any intervention tested on a gifted sample is mathematically prevented from producing medium-or-larger *d*\'s.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** Depends on baseline; **near-ceiling populations cap *d* in the 0.1--0.4 range** regardless of treatment potency.
:::

Cohen 1988  [@cohen1988spa], *Statistical Power Analysis for the Behavioral Sciences* (2nd ed.); Uttl 2005  [@uttl2005], *Psychological Science* 16(6):460--467.

### What the research apparatus adds on top {#what-the-apparatus-adds}

So far the argument has stayed within physics: outcomes that have variance at all, and the bookkeeping that forces causes acting on those outcomes to be small. Real studies do not, however, report the bookkeeping; they report estimates that have passed through a research ecosystem. Six mechanisms describe what the ecosystem adds on top of the physics: two from the reporting filter, four from the ways a single site's estimate fails to generalize. Two of the six admit exact derivations.

#### What the reporting filter does to the estimate {#group-filter .mech-group}

The first two families are physics. This one is not. Even when the underlying effect and the measurement apparatus are honest, the research ecosystem itself adds bias in predictable directions. Studies that reach the literature have passed a significance filter, which inflates the magnitude of the estimates it lets through. Participants who know they are being watched shift their behavior, which inflates the estimate a second time. Both biases attenuate at scale or under unobtrusive measurement, which is why nudges tested by government units produce smaller effects than nudges tested by academics. These bounds tell you not what the world allows, but what the current ecosystem is doing to what you see.

##### The winner\'s curse and regression to the mean {#mech-selection}

Conditioning an estimate on passing \|*t*\| \> 1.96 inflates its magnitude, even with no journal selection.

Investigators apply a significance filter to themselves long before journals do. Conditioning an estimate on passing \|*t*\| \> 1.96 biases its magnitude upward by an amount that depends only on the study's statistical power. Gelman and Carlin 2014 formalize this as the Type M (magnitude) and Type S (sign) error framework. For θ̂ ∼ *N*(θ, *s*²) and δ = θ/*s*,

::: math-display
Power(δ) = Φ(δ − 1.96) + Φ(−δ − 1.96),
:::

::: math-display
Type S(δ) = Φ(−δ − 1.96) / Power(δ),
:::

::: math-display
*E*\[\|θ̂\| \| sig\] = *s* · \[φ(1.96 − δ) + φ(1.96 + δ) + δ (Φ(δ − 1.96) − Φ(−δ − 1.96))\] / Power(δ).
:::

The **Type M ratio** is *E*\[\|θ̂\| \| sig\]/\|θ\|:

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 44. The winner's curse and regression to the mean**

| Power | δ = θ/ s | Type S | Type M |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0.06 | 0.30 | 0.197 | 7.9 |
| 0.10 | 0.65 | 0.045 | 3.7 |
| 0.20 | 1.14 | 0.005 | 2.25 |
| 0.30 | 1.45 | 0.001 | 1.81 |
| 0.50 | 1.96 | <10 −4 | 1.41 |
| 0.80 | 2.80 | <10 −6 | 1.12 |
:::

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG E.** Publication-selection collider. Conditioning on *S* = 1 (passing significance) opens a collider path between true δ and noise ε via *d̂*, so *E*\[\|*d̂*\| \| sig\] \> \|δ\| for all δ.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.* The OSC 2015 empirical shrinkage from mean original *r* ≈ 0.403 to mean replication *r* ≈ 0.197 --- a **2.05× ratio** --- is consistent with implied median power in the 15--20% range. The same factor-of-six DellaVigna--Linos shrinkage shows up again by a different mechanism, suggesting that structural shrinkage factors cluster in the same numerical range across very different sociologies.

The same mechanism repeats at longer timescales. An early extreme estimate, selected for publication precisely because it was extreme, is itself a draw from a distribution of δ̂'s that has a posterior mean much closer to the prior. Formally, for δ ∼ *N*(μ₀, τ²) and δ̂ \| δ ∼ *N*(δ, σ²), the Bayes posterior is *E*\[δ \| δ̂\] = μ₀ + \[τ²/(τ² + σ²)\] (δ̂ − μ₀). A replication draws δ̂~rep~ from that posterior distribution and therefore regresses. The James--Stein result is the strictest statement of the same fact: for *k* ≥ 3 parameters, the MLE is dominated by any shrinkage estimator, even when the parameters are unrelated. Pereira, Horwitz and Ioannidis 2012 examined 85,002 Cochrane forest plots: of 8,239 with a very-large (OR ≥ 5) index trial, the median OR dropped to ≈1.6 when subsequent trials were added, and **exactly one** --- ECMO for neonates --- persisted as a very large effect on a mortality outcome. That's 99.988% regression to the mean by count, and it is not the pathology; it is the arithmetic.

The *decline effect* observed across literatures (Schooler 2011, the "Proteus phenomenon") is the same phenomenon stretched out over the publication history of a finding. Stanley, Carter and Doucouliagos 2018 surveyed 12,065 effect sizes across 200 meta-analyses, found median power at 36%, and found bias-corrected means typically 0.3--0.5× the unadjusted mean. The famous cases read the same way: Bargh, Chen and Burrows 1996's elderly-priming walking-speed effect, originally *d* ≈ 0.5--0.9 on *n* = 30, collapsed to *d* ≈ 0 in Doyen et al. 2012's infrared-timed replication on *n* = 120, with experimenter expectancy accounting for whatever signal remained. Every Type M ratio above row one of the table tells you this story in advance.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** There is no upper bound on *d̂* under selection --- it grows without limit as power → α. The bound is on *credibility*: published *d̂* at typical psychology power (≤ 0.2) should be divided by at least **2.25** to recover an honest point estimate; at power ≈ 0.06, divide by **8--10**.
:::

Gelman & Carlin 2014  [@gelman2014beyondpower], *Perspectives on Psychological Science* 9(6):641--651; van Zwet & Cator 2021  [@zwet2021], *Statistica Neerlandica* 75(4):437--452; Ioannidis 2008  [@ioannidis2008inflation], *Epidemiology* 19(5):640--648; Efron & Morris 1977  [@efron1977stein], *Scientific American* 236(5):119--127; Ioannidis & Trikalinos 2005  [@ioannidis2005proteus], *Journal of Clinical Epidemiology* 58(6):543--549; Pereira, Horwitz & Ioannidis 2012  [@pereira2012largeeffects], *JAMA* 308(16):1676--1684; Schooler 2011  [@schooler2011decline], *Nature* 470:437; Stanley, Carter & Doucouliagos 2018  [@stanley2018metaanalyses], *Psychological Bulletin* 144(12):1325--1346; Bargh, Chen & Burrows 1996  [@bargh1996priming], *JPSP* 71(2):230--244; Doyen et al. 2012  [@doyen2012priming], *PLoS ONE* 7(1):e29081.

##### Hawthorne and demand effects washing out {#mech-demand}

Lab effects that are really "participants knowing they're being watched" vanish at scale. The magnitude of this component is bounded.

Experimental estimates include a "research participation" component --- subjects alter behavior because they know they are observed, or infer what the experimenter wants. At scale, in naturalistic environments, or under unobtrusive observation, this component vanishes. And it is *bounded*: de Quidt, Haushofer & Roth 2018 show that deliberately induced demand shifts behavior by only ≈0.13 SD on average across 11 classic tasks on MTurk. That's a hard ceiling on how much of any lab effect can be Hawthorne artifact.

*Concrete numbers.* de Quidt et al.: average point bound on demand **0.13 SD** (range ≈0.1--0.3); within-subject compliers shift up to 0.77 SD, but the average is 0.13. McCambridge, Witton & Elbourne 2014's review of 19 Hawthorne studies concludes that "there is no single Hawthorne effect": 12 of 19 showed a signal, but heterogeneity was pronounced and direction- and context-dependent. Orne 1962's chilling demonstration: subjects told to add columns of two-digit numbers and tear up each answer sheet continued for 5.5 hours without complaint, assuming the pointlessness was itself the experiment.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** Any published lab *d* \< 0.15 is consistent with being entirely demand artifact. A published *d* = 0.30 leaves a true post-demand *d* ≈ **0.17**; scaled unobtrusive estimates typically land below **0.10**.
:::

de Quidt, Haushofer & Roth 2018  [@dequidt2018demand], *American Economic Review* 108(11):3266--3302; Orne 1962  [@orne1962demand], *American Psychologist* 17(11):776--783; McCambridge, Witton & Elbourne 2014  [@mccambridge2014hawthorne], *Journal of Clinical Epidemiology* 67(3):267--277.

#### Why pilots do not scale {#group-scale .mech-group}

The last family covers why the same intervention, run in a different place or at a different time, does not produce the same number. Real populations are heterogeneous; individual treatment effects vary, and the average the study reports is a specific average that a different population will not match. Group-level associations are not individual-level associations. Identical protocols run in different labs produce different point estimates by irreducible amounts. And the first reports of any effect tend to be larger than the stable mature version, partly because early estimates regress and partly because the easy versions of any problem get solved first. Three of these are physical properties of heterogeneity; the fourth (decline-over-time) is a mix of regression and sociology. All four are why a pilot that worked does not have to scale.

##### Heterogeneity absorbs the average {#mech-heterogeneity}

Interventions that work for some people and harm others can produce ATEs near zero with large individual effects underneath.

The average treatment effect a study reports is a summary of the individual treatment effect distribution across people, contexts, and moments. When that distribution has substantial dispersion, large positive effects in some subgroups cancel against null or negative effects in others, producing a small ATE even when the intervention is powerful for specific subpopulations. With τ*~i~* ∼ (τ̄, σ~τ~²), outcome noise σ², and random assignment,

::: math-display
ATE = τ̄,   Var(ATÊ) = (2σ² + σ~τ~²)/*n*.
:::

If τ*~i~* = τ₀ + τ₁ *W~i~* with *E*\[*W*\] = 0, the ATE = τ₀ is invariant under moderator symmetry while \|τ*~i~*\| can be arbitrarily large --- the ATE can be zero while every individual is materially affected. Detecting an interaction of the same magnitude as a main effect typically requires 16× the sample size (Gelman 2018).

::: dag-fig
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

::: dag-caption
**DAG F.** Moderation / effect modification. The *X* → *Y* edge's coefficient depends on the level of the moderator *W*. The ATE is the population average of a heterogeneous individual effect.
:::
:::

*Concrete numbers.* Toy nudge scenario: *d* = 0.8 for 20% of the population, *d* = 0 for 60%, *d* = −0.3 for 20%. ATE = 0.10. *E*\[\|τ*~i~*\|\] = 0.22. SD(τ*~i~*) ≈ 0.37. Kenny & Judd 2019's critique: the field's preference for single mega-trials over distributed replication is strategically misguided. With any genuine between-site τ, no single study --- however large --- generalizes. The fix is distributed multi-site work, not bigger single sites.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** When σ~τ~ is comparable to \|τ̄\|, the ATE can shrink to 0.1 while individual \|*d~i~*\| averages 0.4 --- a factor-of-four absorption. For most behavioral interventions, **ATE ceilings of *d* ≈ 0.1--0.3** coexist with much larger CATEs for responsive subgroups.
:::

Bryan, Tipton & Yeager 2021  [@bryan2021heterogeneity], *Nature Human Behaviour* 5(8):980--989; Kenny & Judd 2019  [@kenny2019heterogeneity], *Psychological Methods* 24(5):578--589; Olsson-Collentine, Wicherts & van Assen 2020  [@olssoncollentine2020], *Psychological Bulletin* 146(10):922--940; Gelman 2018  [@gelman2018interactions], blog post, Statistical Modeling.

##### Compositional and ecological effects {#mech-ecological}

Group-level correlations aren't individual-level correlations. The same data can give opposite answers.

Correlations computed at group level can differ in magnitude or sign from correlations at individual level. Designs that mix within- and between-person variance produce a published effect that is a weighted mixture of two structurally different covariances. Interventions targeted on the basis of ecological associations therefore hit a much smaller --- sometimes oppositely-signed --- individual-level parameter. Decomposing *Y~ij~* and *X~ij~* into between- and within-cluster components,

::: math-display
Cov(*Y*, *X*) = Cov(α~j~, β~j~) + *E~j~*\[Cov(ε, η \| *j*)\].
:::

Simpson's paradox occurs whenever those two correlations differ in sign.

*Concrete numbers.* Robinson 1950 on 1930 US census data: state-level correlation between percent foreign-born and literacy was **+0.53**; individual-level correlation was **−0.11**. Modern analogues include Gelman's red-state / blue-state income--vote inversion and the Berkeley 1973 admissions paradox (Bickel, Hammel & O'Connell 1975): the university appeared to discriminate against women overall while every department admitted women at equal or higher rates. Molenaar's ergodicity theorem: between-person equals within-person only if the process is stationary and homogeneous --- conditions almost never met in psychology.

::: ceiling-box
**Implied ceiling.** An ecological correlation *r* = 0.5 (*d* ≈ 1.15) with within-cluster *r* = 0.1 yields individual-level *d* ≈ 0.2; under sign-flip it yields zero or negative. Rule of thumb: multiply the naive *d* by the between-to-total variance ratio, typically **0.05--0.20** in cluster-sampled social data.
:::

Robinson 1950  [@robinson1950ecological], *American Sociological Review* 15(3):351--357; Kievit, Frankenhuis, Waldorp & Borsboom 2013  [@kievit2013simpson], *Frontiers in Psychology* 4:513; Molenaar 2004  [@molenaar2004], *Measurement* 2(4):201--218; Bickel, Hammel & O'Connell 1975  [@bickel1975berkeley], *Science* 187(4175):398--404.

##### Site-to-site heterogeneity and voltage drop at scale {#mech-multisite}

Even with identical pre-registered protocols, effect sizes differ across sites by irreducible amounts.

Even under identical pre-registered protocols, true effects vary across sites due to latent moderators (population, language, experimenter, stimuli micro-variation).
